name: Katex.Configs.Math
comment:
You should use this module only if you need `m`ath configurations.

_Understand `Katex` before reading on._

### Why is this module necessary?

For example, let's say you are not sure which of `\phi` and `\varphi` you prefer.
In this case, you can write code with `\phi` which may be replaced depending on your configuration. See _§Katex.Configs.Math_ in the README for this example in action.

# Types
@docs Latex

# Create LaTeX instance
@docs human, inline, display

# Process LaTeX instances
@docs print, generate

values.name: display
values.comment: Like `Katex.display`, but instead of feeding it a string, you feed it a `m`ath-dependent string.

values.name: generate
values.comment: Like `Katex.generate`, but depends on the `m`ath configurations. The generating function has access to the `m`ath configurations, but this is usually unnecessary, in which case simply use a generating function which ignores its first argument.
values.type: (m -> Maybe Bool -> String -> a) -> m -> Latex m -> a

values.name: human
values.comment: Like `Katex.human`.

values.name: inline
values.comment: Like `Katex.inline`, but instead of feeding it a string, you feed it a `m`ath-dependent string.

values.name: print
values.comment: Like `Katex.print`, but depends on the `m`ath configurations.

name: Katex

values.name: display
values.comment: Use `display` when writing LaTeX code for display math environment.

values.name: generate
values.comment:
Generate a function over LaTeX values. The boolean value represents whether the math is in display mode, i.e.

* `Nothing` for human
* `Just False` for inline
* `Just True` for display

For example, let's say you want an `Html a` emitting function which puts display math in a `div`, but inline math and human text in a `span`.

    view : Latex -> Html a
    view =
        let
            htmlGenerator isDisplayMode stringLatex =
                case isDisplayMode of
                    Just True ->
                        H.div [] [ H.text stringLatex ]
                    _ ->
                        H.span [] [ H.text stringLatex ]
        in
            generate htmlGenerator

Another example is the built-in `print` function.

    print : Latex -> String
    print =
        generate (\_ stringLatex -> stringLatex)

values.name: human
values.comment: Use `human` when writing regular text.

values.name: inline
values.comment: Use `inline` when writing LaTeX code for inline math environment.

values.name: print
values.comment: Turn a LaTeX instance into a string that the KaTeX library recognizes.

name: Katex.Configs

values.name: display
values.comment: Like `Katex.Configs.Math.display`.

values.name: generate
values.comment: Like `Katex.generate`, but depends on the `m`ath and `h`uman configurations. The generating function has access to the `m`ath and `h`uman configurations, but this is usually unnecessary, in which case simply use a generating function which ignores its first arguments.
values.type: (m -> h -> Maybe Bool -> String -> a) -> m -> h -> Latex m h -> a

values.name: human
values.comment: Like `Katex.Configs.Human.human`.

values.name: inline
values.comment: Like `Katex.Configs.Math.inline`.

values.name: print
values.comment: Like `Katex.print`, but depends on the `m`ath and `h`uman configurations.
values.type: m -> h -> Latex m h -> String
